symbols: chalice — the Eucharistic cup, the blood of Christ shed for the remission of sins
IHS monogram — Christogram representing the Holy Name of Jesus
angels in adoration — heavenly worship before the sacramental presence of Christ
wings — celestial nature and divine messengers
architectural canopy — ciborium, tabernacle, heavenly sanctuary
red glass — Precious Blood, divine love
description: Two winged angels stand facing each other in attitudes of prayer and adoration. Between them, slightly elevated on a pedestal or altar structure, rests a large chalice surmounted by a circular host bearing a cross. The angel on the left wears richly patterned red and gold vestments; the angel on the right wears white. Both have large feathered wings in warm tones. Above the chalice rises an architectural canopy with a tiled roof suggesting a tabernacle or sanctuary. The background glows with deep blues, vivid reds, and luminous whites, framing the Eucharistic elements as the focal point of heavenly veneration.
theological_description: This window presents a deeply Eucharistic theology. The two angels adoring the chalice express the doctrine that the heavenly host worships Christ present in the Eucharist, reflecting the belief that every Mass unites the earthly liturgy with the heavenly worship described in Revelation 4-5. The IHS monogram above the chalice identifies the sacramental contents as Christ himself, pointing to the theology of the Real Presence. The chalice directly recalls the Last Supper (Luke 22:20), where Jesus took the cup and said, 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood.' The two angels echo the cherubim who flanked the Ark of the Covenant (Exodus 25:18-22), the mercy seat being a type of the Eucharistic altar where God's presence dwells among his people. The scene also evokes Isaiah 6:1-7, where seraphim attend the throne of God in the heavenly temple, and one brings a burning coal to purify the prophet's lips -- a patristic type for Holy Communion. The Melchizedek offering of bread and wine (Genesis 14:18) prefigures the Eucharistic sacrifice, while Psalm 116:13 declares 'I will lift up the cup of salvation and call upon the name of the Lord.'

scripture_refs.reason: The chalice directly represents the cup of the New Covenant in Christ's blood instituted at the Last Supper
Two cherubim flanking the mercy seat on the Ark prefigure the angels adoring Christ's Eucharistic presence
Heavenly beings falling in worship before the Lamb parallels the angelic adoration of the Eucharist
Seraphim attending God's throne and the coal touching Isaiah's lips as a type of sacramental communion
Christ as High Priest entering the heavenly sanctuary with his own blood, represented by the chalice
Paul's account of the institution of the Eucharistic cup and proclamation of the Lord's death
Melchizedek's offering of bread and wine prefigures the Eucharistic sacrifice
The cup of salvation lifted up in thanksgiving to the Lord
